summary:
The Plantation System in the Americas (c. 1600–1800 AD) was the large-scale agricultural regime based on enslaved African labor that dominated the tropical and subtropical colonies of Britain, France, Spain, Portugal, and the Netherlands, producing export commodities — sugar, tobacco, cotton, indigo, rice — that drove the commercial economy of the [[event:2341|Atlantic slave trade]] and enriched European metropoles. The plantation system emerged after initial attempts to use Indigenous American labor failed through epidemic disease and resistance; landowners turned to enslaved Africans as a labor force that could be coerced, transported, and reproduced across generations. By the 18th century, plantation agriculture dominated the Caribbean islands, the coastal American South, Brazil, and parts of Central America.

The plantation regime was characterized by extreme violence and dehumanization. Enslaved people worked under brutal conditions — particularly in sugar cultivation, where the labor of cutting cane and processing it in mills was so grueling that mortality rates in sugar colonies regularly exceeded birth rates, requiring constant importation of new enslaved people. The racial ideology that developed to justify chattel slavery — asserting the natural inferiority and subhumanity of Africans — became deeply embedded in law, culture, and science. The plantation system generated enormous wealth: by 1770, the small island of Saint-Domingue (Haiti) produced more sugar and coffee than all of Spanish America combined, making it France's most valuable colony. This wealth also funded Enlightenment culture in European cities, creating a fundamental contradiction between the ideals of liberty articulated in the [[event:2346|Age of Enlightenment]] and the enslaved labor on which European prosperity in part rested.
